layers.description: The brain experiences mild psychological discomfort when it detects an information gap — the space between what it knows and what it wants to know. This discomfort creates a compulsive drive to close the gap.
layers.application: Curiosity hooks, open loops, and withheld revelations all exploit information gap theory. The hook creates the gap; the content closes it. Never close the gap before the reader has fully committed to reading.

layers.description: From prospect theory (Kahneman & Tversky): losses are neurologically weighted approximately twice as heavily as equivalent gains. People work harder to avoid losing $100 than to gain $100.
layers.application: Frame inaction as loss, not missed opportunity. 'You're losing $300/month by not doing this' outperforms 'You could gain $300/month by doing this.' Use sparingly — chronic loss framing creates anxiety and disengagement.

layers.description: In conditions of uncertainty, the brain uses others' behavior (social proof) and credentialed sources (authority bias) as cognitive shortcuts for determining correct action.
layers.application: Specific numbers outperform vague claims. '47,000 founders' > 'thousands of founders.' Named institutions > unnamed experts. Real testimonials with full names > anonymous quotes.

layers.description: People make decisions that are consistent with their self-concept. Copy that speaks to who someone is (or wants to become) bypasses feature-benefit evaluation and activates identity-protective cognition.
layers.application: Lead with identity, not solution. 'For people who believe writing is a competitive advantage' works because it activates belonging before it proposes a product.

layers.description: The brain's working memory is limited. Every unnecessary word, complex sentence, or unclear structure consumes cognitive resources that could be spent on persuasion. Simplicity isn't dumbing down — it's freeing up bandwidth for conversion.
layers.application: Short sentences. Active voice. One idea per paragraph. The most important idea first. Every edit should increase clarity or increase persuasive force — preferably both.
